dialogue.utterance: Unit 4, responding to a 71-year-old female found unresponsive at an address in Richmond. Call type: Unconscious/Fainting/Near-Fainting.
Hi, I'm Emily, an EMT with the rescue squad. We heard you were feeling unwell. What made 911 called for her?
She was found on the floor this morning and hasn't been responsive since.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes for me? Can you speak or move your fingers?
There's no eye movement-she appears unresponsive.
She's not making any sounds or reacting verbally.
No motor response as well-extremities are flaccid and show no purposeful movement.
Partner, can you get the suction kit? She may have secretions in her airway.
Got it-starting suction now.
Airway is partially clear after suctioning-secretions were visible in the back of the throat.
Resp rate is 22-mild snoring but otherwise regular.
Breathing appears steady and not labored-no signs of distress observed at this time.
Lungs are sounds without focal issue-working on confirming bilateral breath sounds.
Spo2 increased from 84 to 95 on a non-rebreather mask after we secured the airway.
Pulse is 51-strong and bradycardic, but patient is not in acute distress.
Patient is pale and cool to the touch-no visible cyanosis or excessive sweating.
Peripheral pulses are poor-she's been on the floor for a while.
Check the pupils and let me know what you see.
Right pupil is 3mm and fixed-left is 5mm and fixed as well. No response to light.
Secretions noted in the back of the throat-suggesting partial airway obstruction prior to intervention.
Airway is now clear and secured-can you confirm no signs of head trauma or bleeding at face/nose/mouth?
No evidence of visible trauma or bleeding on the head or face-mouth and nose are clean.
Skin is cool, pale, but within accepted norms-with no visible rashes or signs of poor turgor.
BP is now 196 over 129-higher than initial reading of 186/82, documenting unusual hypertensive pattern.
Heart rate remains at 51, slow but steady-confirmed bradycardia post-suctioning and IV.
Respiratory rate at 22-stable and regular.
She's at 95% now with the NRB-SpO2 improved after airway and oxygen intervention.
EKG remains consistent with sinus bradycardia as per 4 lead read, matching baseline.
Getting ready to start IV fluids-she takes Warfarin but no other known allergies.
IV started successfully in the left forearm after two attempts.
Nasopharyngeal airway placed in the right nare-airway remains clear and patent.
Starting 6L oxygen via nasal cannula-will increase to 15L NRB if Spo2 drops slightly.
She's showing Cushing's signs-untreated increased intracranial pressure. Suspect head bleed.
Agreed-transporting her directly to VCU for stroke alert and neuro consultation.
VCU is the closest trauma center with stroke alert capability-do we have consent Bystander?
Yes, VCU is the right choice for her.
Stretcher locked and securing her with straps-airway and IV remain stable.
Airway is stable, breathing good, and oxygen is sufficient. Pulse maintains bradycardia.
IV in good position and flowing-no red flags in transport.
SpO2 remains at 95% on 15L NRB-patient seems stable on transport vitals.
BP rechecked at 189 over 72-Pulse 55-all consistent with earlier readings on scene.
VCU is aware and trauma team is on standby-ETA is under 7 minutes.
VCU ED trauma team is ready and preparing for the patient's arrival-all key info shared.
All updated vitals and interventions are transferred with the patient-she is ready for arrival.
Pulse 51-Spo2 91-BP 189 over 72-values remain unchanged.
She's down and secure on the stretcher-preparing for en route.
Unequal fixed pupils indicate Cushing's-need to stress hospital for immediate brain imaging.
12 lead ECG obtained and reviewed-no MI detected at this time.
No new changes with the 12 lead read-still sinus bradycardia.
She remains stable-GCS 3 with no changes during or after interventions.
She takes Warfarin, Tramadol, and Lasix-other medical history isn't available.
No other medications-she was physically okay at least yesterday evening.
No reported allergies-home health records don't list any.
Minor falls before, no major surgery-conditions based on prescribed meds.
Last food intake though not certain, was likely last night.
Found unresponsive-nearby no signs of recent fall or injury.
Still flaccid with no motor response-deep stimuli show no reaction.
No sensory response observed-she continues to be minimally responsive.
BP, Pulse, and SpO2 are all still stable-we're good to head out.
She's ready for transport-no further interventions needed before leaving.
Transferred safely using Reeves-IV remains secure, airway maintained.
BP 189/72-SpO2 at 91-Pulse at 55-consistently stable for arrival.
4 lead ECG ongoing, 12 lead sent with transport-ED will interpret final read.
Glucose was unobtainable-showed 0 due to unresponsiveness, no action taken.
We followed Stroke/TIA Protocol 3-5-based on suspected intracranial hemorrhage.
No poor turgor-cool due to prolonged positional time, not dehydrated.
VCU is 5 minutes away-quick response and smooth traffic conditions.
All assessments unchanged-airway, IV, vitals remain stable in transit.
All monitoring intact with no anomalies-readings unchanged since departure.
Pulse remains steady at 51-no acute changes indicate deterioration.
Neurological exam still at GCS 3-no immediate improvement or decline noted.
Monitors working as expected-logging pulse, SpO2, BP, and EKG during transport.
EKG is still reading sinus bradycardia-no signs of acute MI.
VCU alerted with key vitals and suspected hemorrhage-will require head imaging on arrival.
Recommend immediate head imaging-possible subdural or subarachnoid hemorrhage.
ePCR finalized and provided to ED-she's been transported as a stroke alert.
All interventions documented-transport checks complete.
Arrival ahead-expect smooth transfer of care at the VCU trauma bay.
IV line remains stable-ED to take over as soon as she is handed off.
Handover fully done-patient and all pertinent info now with ED trauma team.
All steps complete-safe arrival expected at VCU with handoff done.
Final vitals as per pre-transfer-BP 189/72-SpO2 91-Pulse 55.
